codeSnapshot.label: batch_norm.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Batch Normalisation in a CNN
model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 64, 3, padding=1),
    nn.BatchNorm2d(64),   # after conv, before activation
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Conv2d(64, 128, 3, padding=1),
    nn.BatchNorm2d(128),
    nn.ReLU()
)

# Manual BatchNorm (1D for clarity)
def batch_norm_manual(x, gamma, beta, eps=1e-5):
    # x: (B, D)
    mu = x.mean(dim=0)            # (D,) — mean across batch
    var = x.var(dim=0, unbiased=False)  # (D,) — variance across batch
    x_hat = (x - mu) / (var + eps).sqrt()  # normalise
    return gamma * x_hat + beta   # scale and shift

B, D = 32, 256
x = torch.randn(B, D)
gamma = torch.ones(D)   # learnable scale, init=1
beta = torch.zeros(D)   # learnable shift, init=0
x_out = batch_norm_manual(x, gamma, beta)
print(f"After BN: mean={x_out.mean():.4f}, std={x_out.std():.4f}")  # ≈ 0, 1

# At inference: use running statistics (not batch stats)
# PyTorch BN tracks: running_mean, running_var
# running_mean = (1-momentum)*running_mean + momentum*batch_mean  (momentum=0.1)
bn = nn.BatchNorm1d(D)
bn.train()  # uses batch statistics
bn.eval()   # uses running_mean, running_var

# Batch size effect
# Large B: stable statistics (BN works well)
# Small B (B=1,2): unstable statistics — use LayerNorm or GroupNorm

eli3: Batch Normalisation (BatchNorm) is a technique that normalises the inputs to each layer so they always have approximately zero mean and unit variance, within each mini-batch. This keeps the input distribution to each layer stable throughout training, so each layer doesn't have to constantly readjust to shifts in its input.

Without BatchNorm, as the weights in early layers change, the distribution of inputs to later layers shifts constantly — called 'internal covariate shift'. This forces later layers to keep re-learning how to handle different input distributions, slowing training. BatchNorm removes this problem.

BatchNorm has two learnable parameters per unit: γ (scale) and β (shift). After normalising to zero mean and unit variance, these learned parameters let the network re-scale if needed. So BatchNorm doesn't force the network to be normalised — it just provides a stable normalised starting point that it can adapt from.

whenWhy.title: Training deep CNNs (ResNet, VGG, EfficientNet)
whenWhy.body: BatchNorm is the standard regularisation technique for convolutional networks. It allows training with higher learning rates (normalised activations prevent gradient explosion), reduces sensitivity to weight initialisation, and acts as a regulariser (mini-batch statistics add noise similar to dropout). ResNet-50 without BN trains poorly; with BN it achieves 76% ImageNet top-1.

whenWhy.title: Stabilising training with high learning rates
whenWhy.body: Ioffe & Szegedy (2015) showed BatchNorm allows 14× higher learning rates without divergence. Normalised inputs limit the effective gradient scale — even if weights in a layer are very large or small, the normalised output has controlled magnitude. This makes the loss landscape smoother and allows larger, faster gradient steps.

whenWhy.title: When BatchNorm fails: small batches, RNNs, or online learning
whenWhy.body: BatchNorm's statistics are unstable with batch size B < 8 (high variance estimate). Alternative: LayerNorm (normalise over features, not batch — works for B=1) for transformers and RNNs; GroupNorm (normalise over channel groups) for object detection where batch size varies; InstanceNorm (normalise per sample per channel) for style transfer.

deepDiveIntro: BatchNorm (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015): given a mini-batch {xᵢ} of B samples, for each feature dimension d: μ_d = (1/B)Σᵢ xᵢ,d; σ²_d = (1/B)Σᵢ(xᵢ,d − μ_d)²; x̂ᵢ,d = (xᵢ,d − μ_d)/√(σ²_d + ε); yᵢ,d = γ_d · x̂ᵢ,d + β_d. Parameters γ, β are learned. At training: use batch statistics. At inference: use exponential moving averages of training statistics: μ_run ← (1−m)μ_run + m·μ_batch (PyTorch default m=0.1). [1]

The gradient of BatchNorm has a peculiar structure: the gradient ∂L/∂xᵢ depends on all other samples in the batch via the shared μ and σ². This mini-batch coupling is both BatchNorm's strength (smoothing) and limitation (batch-size dependence, incompatibility with sequential models). [1]

deepDive.heading: Forward Pass Algorithm
deepDive.body: Given mini-batch B = {x₁,...,x_B}: μ = (1/B)Σxᵢ. σ² = (1/B)Σ(xᵢ−μ)². x̂ᵢ = (xᵢ−μ)/√(σ²+ε). y = γx̂ + β. ε (typically 1e-5) prevents division by zero. γ (init=1) and β (init=0) are learned via gradient descent with the rest of the network. The normalisation is applied per-feature (for FC layers) or per-channel (for conv layers, averaging over batch AND spatial dimensions). [1]
deepDive.formula: x̂ = (x − μ_B) / √(σ²_B + ε);  y = γx̂ + β

deepDive.heading: Effect on Gradient Flow
deepDive.body: Normalisation makes the gradient w.r.t. pre-normalisation activations scale-invariant: if you scale all weights in a layer by c, the output after BN is unchanged (c cancels in normalisation). But the gradient through BN also changes: ∂L/∂W is proportional to 1/‖W‖, so gradients from BN-normalised layers effectively have adaptive learning rates. This is why BN allows higher lr — the actual gradient signal is self-normalising. [1]

deepDive.heading: Batch Norm in Inference Mode
deepDive.body: At inference (model.eval()), PyTorch uses running_mean and running_var instead of batch statistics. running_mean is updated during training as: running_mean ← (1 − momentum)·running_mean + momentum·batch_mean (PyTorch: momentum=0.1 by default, so running mean tracks with 10-step lag). Forgetting to call model.eval() is a common bug: inference predictions will use noisy batch statistics from a single sample, producing unstable outputs. Always model.eval() before inference. [1]

deepDive.heading: BatchNorm vs LayerNorm vs GroupNorm
deepDive.body: Normalisation axis: BatchNorm: average over batch dimension (axis=0). LayerNorm: average over feature dimension (axis=-1). GroupNorm: average over groups of channels (axis=c within groups). InstanceNorm: average over spatial dimensions per-sample per-channel. Use case: BatchNorm for CNNs (B≥16); LayerNorm for transformers and NLP (B=1 works); GroupNorm for object detection (variable B); InstanceNorm for style transfer (per-image normalisation). [2]

takeaways: Normalise mini-batch to μ=0, σ²=1; then learn γ, β to re-scale: y=γ((x−μ)/√(σ²+ε))+β. [1]
Training: batch statistics; inference: running mean/var (call model.eval()!). [1]
Allows 14× higher learning rates; reduces init sensitivity; implicit regularisation. [1]
Fails for small batch sizes (B<8) or sequential models — use LayerNorm instead. [2]
GroupNorm for object detection; InstanceNorm for style transfer. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does BatchNorm act as a regulariser?
edgeCases.a: BatchNorm's statistics (μ_B, σ²_B) depend on the random mini-batch composition. Different batches produce different normalisation statistics for each sample — the same sample x_i receives slightly different normalisation depending on which other samples are in its batch. This is random noise analogous to dropout: each sample experiences a slightly different transformation during training. At test time, fixed running statistics remove this noise. This mini-batch dependence is a stochastic regularisation that reduces overfitting — empirically, models trained with BN often need less dropout.

edgeCases.q: What goes wrong when you use BatchNorm with very small batch sizes?
edgeCases.a: With B=1 (single sample), the sample's own statistics (mean and variance computed from d features) are used. Variance estimation with one sample has high variance itself — you're estimating population statistics from a single observation. For B=2: estimation is still poor. The variance estimate has E[σ²_batch] = σ²_pop·(1−1/B) bias. For B=2: σ²_batch ≈ 0.5σ²_pop — systematically underestimates variance, causing over-normalisation. For detection models (DETR, Faster-RCNN) where B=2 is common due to GPU memory, GroupNorm (G=32 groups, normalises within groups of channels) is used instead.

sources.label: Ioffe & Szegedy (2015) — Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training arXiv:1502.03167
